senses.definition: used to introduce an explanation or a reason for something; often a way of clarifying a situation

examples.x: The thing is, my parents like me to be home by ten o'clock.
I’d love to go, but the thing is, I have a prior commitment.
We could go out, but the thing is, it’s already getting late.
The thing is, I’ve never been to that restaurant before.
She agreed to meet, but the thing is, she’s running late.

senses.definition: used before a comment or opinion to indicate that the speaker is sharing it, even though they may not be sure of its value or significance

examples.x: I'm not an expert on the matter, but for what it's worth, I think you should consult a professional.
For what it's worth, I think you should consider taking a different approach to this project.
I know you're not convinced, but for what it's worth, I believe giving it a try could lead to some positive outcomes.
For what it's worth, I've heard mixed reviews about that restaurant, but I'm open to giving it a shot if you're up for it.
I understand your hesitation, but for what it's worth, I think you're more capable of handling this challenge than you realize.

senses.definition: to refuse to change one's opinions, behaviors, habits, etc.

examples.x: Despite the advancements in technology, my grandfather remains set in his ways and prefers to stick to his traditional methods.
She's been living in the same neighborhood for decades and has become set in her ways, resisting any suggestions for change.
The company's management team was criticized for being set in their ways and unwilling to adopt innovative strategies.
After years of following a strict routine, he has become set in his ways and finds it difficult to adapt to sudden changes.
Despite the feedback from colleagues, he remains set in his ways and refuses to consider alternative viewpoints.

senses.definition: to express one's opinions in an honest or fearless way

examples.x: I'm not afraid to speak my mind, even if it means ruffling some feathers.
The politician was known for his willingness to speak his mind, even when it meant going against the party line.
The outspoken activist was not afraid to speak her mind about the injustices she witnessed.
The child was always honest to a fault, never afraid to speak his mind, even to adults.

senses.definition: to adopt a different opinion or point of view, particularly a contradictory one

word_list.pronunciation: θˈɪŋk lˈɪɾəl bˈædli pˈʊɹli ʌv ˌɛsbˈiː

senses.definition: to have a negative or unfavorable opinion or perception of that person

examples.x: The manager's constant criticism made the employees think poorly of him.
The teacher's dismissive attitude towards the students made them think badly of her.
The politician's lack of integrity made many people think little of him.

senses.definition: to think that something is normal, easy, unimportant, etc., particularly while others think it to be wrong, strange, difficult, important, etc.
to think in a creative way in order to come up with original solutions

examples.x: The team always thinks outside the box to come up with unique advertisements for all its clients.
To solve this complex puzzle, we need to think outside the box and explore unconventional approaches.
The scientist made a groundbreaking discovery by thinking outside the box and challenging established theories.
The entrepreneur succeeded in the competitive market by thinking outside the box and identifying unmet customer needs.

senses.definition: to perceive or regard someone or something with a new perspective, particularly one that is positive

examples.x: After attending the conference, I saw the topic of climate change in a new light, realizing the urgency and importance of taking action.
After reading her biography, I saw her accomplishments in a different light and gained a deeper admiration for her resilience and determination.
The documentary presented the controversial issue in a different light, challenging commonly held beliefs and inviting viewers to reconsider their opinions.
Traveling to a foreign country allowed me to see my own culture and values in a new light, gaining a broader perspective and appreciation for diversity.
After hearing her personal story, I came to see her in a new light, understanding her motivations and struggles better.

senses.definition: used to advise someone to trust one's judgment or experience concerning a particular matter

examples.x: Take it from me, learning to code is not as difficult as it seems.
Take it from me, raising children is a challenging yet rewarding experience.
Take it from me, traveling to new places opens your mind and broadens your perspective.
Take it from me, investing in your health is one of the most important things you can do.
Take it from me, pursuing your passions leads to a fulfilling and meaningful life.

senses.definition: used to tell someone to accept the word or advice of someone else, particularly someone who is older or more experienced

examples.x: Take it from someone who's been there, learning a new language takes time and effort, but it's definitely achievable.
As a seasoned entrepreneur, I can take it from someone that starting a business is not for the faint of heart, but it can be incredibly rewarding.
I've been a teacher for over 20 years, so take it from someone who knows, every student has the potential to succeed.
As a former athlete, I can take it from someone who's experienced the pressure of competition, that mental toughness is crucial for success in any field.
Having traveled the world extensively, I can take it from someone who's experienced different cultures, that open-mindedness and adaptability are essential for a fulfilling life.

senses.definition: a belief that is strong, yet without any explainable reason

examples.x: I had a gut feeling that the job interview was going well, and I was right.
My gut reaction to the new restaurant was that it was overpriced and overrated.
The detective had a gut feeling that the suspect was guilty, even though there was no concrete evidence
The athlete trusted her gut feeling when it came to making game-time decisions.

senses.definition: used before advising someone or giving them a warning

examples.x: A word to the wise: always check the expiration date before consuming food or medication.
A word to the wise: avoid swimming in unfamiliar waters, especially if there are posted warnings or lifeguards are absent.
A word to the wise: be cautious when sharing personal information online, as it can be misused or fall into the wrong hands.
A word to the wise: save money regularly, even small amounts, as it can accumulate over time and provide financial security in the future.
A word to the wise: treat others with kindness and respect, as you never know when you might need their help or support.

senses.definition: (of a feeling, problem, or belief) to be really strong or troublesome due to having existed for a long time

examples.x: The scars of war run deep in the hearts of the survivors, who still bear the physical and emotional wounds of conflict.
The roots of prejudice run deep in the fabric of society, shaping cultural norms and influencing individual attitudes.
The fear of failure runs deep in the minds of many people, holding them back from pursuing their dreams and taking risks.
The tradition of hospitality runs deep in this culture, as visitors are welcomed with open arms and treated with warmth and kindness.
The desire for freedom runs deep in the human spirit, a yearning for self-determination and the ability to live life on one's own terms.

senses.definition: saying what is in one's mind in a very forceful yet honest manner

examples.x: The manager gave his employees straight from the shoulder feedback, praising their strengths and identifying areas for improvement.
The politician was known for her straight from the shoulder speeches, addressing controversial issues without hesitation or equivocation.
The therapist encouraged her patient to be straight from the shoulder with her family, expressing her feelings openly and honestly.
The teacher appreciated her students' straight from the shoulder questions, fostering a classroom environment of open dialogue and intellectual curiosity.
The friend gave her straight from the shoulder advice, pointing out the potential consequences of her actions without judgment or malice.

word_list.pronunciation: tʊ ðə bˈɛst ʌv maɪ nˈɑːlɪdʒ

senses.definition: used when one is about to say something that is only based on information they have

examples.x: To my knowledge, there have been no reported cases of food poisoning from this restaurant.
To the best of my knowledge, the company has not yet implemented any new policies regarding employee conduct.
To my knowledge, the historical records do not mention any significant battles fought in this region.
To the best of my knowledge, the scientific consensus supports the theory of evolution.
To my knowledge, there are no known side effects associated with this medication.

senses.definition: to continuously talk about something, in a way that seems foolish or careless

examples.x: The child got into trouble for shooting off his mouth in class, making rude comments and interrupting the teacher
The young man was known for shooting off his mouth, often making inappropriate or offensive remarks without thinking.
The impulsive teenager often shot off his mouth without thinking, leading to arguments and misunderstandings with friends and family.
The social media influencer's habit of shooting off her mouth about controversial topics caused her to lose followers and damage her reputation.

senses.definition: in a state in which one is disliked by someone

examples.x: The child's constant tantrums, her disrespectful attitude towards her parents, and her refusal to obey their rules caused a rift between her and her parents, putting her firmly in their bad books.

senses.definition: in a state in which someone is very pleased with one
to change one's opinion or behavior toward something

examples.x: He bragged that the test was easy, but when he saw his grade he changed his tune.
He soon changed his tune when he saw how angry I was.
He was against the idea to start with, but he soon changed his tune when he realized how much money he'd get.
The question is, will the president change his tune on taxes?
Wait until it happens to him—he'll soon change his tune.

senses.definition: to have an understanding of how one is viewed or valued by another person

examples.x: The employee sought feedback from the manager to know where they stood regarding their contributions to the team.
Following the team meeting, everyone knew where they stood on the new project, clarifying roles and expectations.

word_list.pronunciation: nˌɑːɾɐv ɐ ɡˈʊd wˈɜːd tə sˈeɪ fɔːɹ ɔːɹ ɐbˌaʊt ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ

senses.definition: to have a very negative opinion about someone or something and to be unable to say anything positive or complimentary about them

examples.x: The movie reviewer was so bored by the film that he didn't have a good word to say for it, calling it a waste of time and money.
He doesn’t have a good word to say about his old job; he always complains about it.

senses.definition: to refrain from speaking or expressing one's thoughts or opinions

examples.x: Despite his frustration, he held his tongue during the meeting to avoid causing further conflict.
It took all of his willpower to hold his tongue when he heard the offensive remarks.
Even though he knew he was right, he decided to hold his tongue and let the matter drop.
The politician held his tongue during the debate, carefully choosing his words to avoid controversy.
In situations like these, it's often better to hold your tongue and listen rather than speak impulsively.
